english: sincere belief, that they will acquire thereby higher gifts and abilities, by others merely to increase their odour of sanctity. mtsams sdóm-pa (mtshams sdom pa) Mil. has a similar signification. — spyad-mtsáms (spyad mtshams) rules, instructions, defining the extent and limits of a person's duties. — 4. symb. num.: 6, v. mtsams drug (mtshams drug) above.

1. fair, fine, beautiful, = mdzés-pa (mdzes pa) Zam., Glr. frq., mtsar sdug daṅ ldán-pa (mtshar sdug dang ldan pa) id., e. g. bú-mo (bu mo) Glr.; also of flowers; bright, shining, of metals Stg.; *nyám-tsar-wa* (nyam mtshar ba), *ló-tsar-wa* (blo mtshar ba) admirably fair, wonderfully fine. — 2. wondrous, wonderful, marvelous, gen. with ṅo (ngo), ṅo-mtsar-càn źig (ngo mtshar can zhig) a wonderful, distinguished, eminent man Mil.; rten ṅo-mtsar-càn (rten ngo mtshar can) a wonderful image (of some deity) Glr., in both instances equivalent to wonder-working, miraculous; ṅo-mtsar-mcód-pa (ngo mtshar mchod pa) a marvelous, extremely rich offering Mil.; more frq. ṅo-mtsar-cé-ba (ngo mtshar che ba) e.g. marvelous things, events, miracles Dzl.; mi srid ṅo-mtsar-ce (mi srid ngo mtshar che) impossible! most wonderful! Glr.; ṅo-mtsar-cé-ba ma yin (ngo mtshar che ba ma yin) that is not so very wonderful Dzl.; strange, ridiculous, rtam śin-tu ṅo-mtsar-cé (gtam shin tu ngo mtshar che) Glr. — 3. ṅo-mtsár (ngo mtshar) wonder, surprise, astonishment, ṅo-mtsár skyé-ba (ngo mtshar skye ba), ṅo-mtsár-du °gyur-ba (ngo mtshar du 'gyur ba) or °dzin-pa ('dzin pa), ṅo-mtsar-rmád-du °gyur-ba (ngo mtshar rmad du 'gyur ba) to wonder, to be surprised. — 4. ṅo-mtar-cé (ngo mtshar che) an expression of thanks, = bka-drin-cé (bka' drin che), dé-ltar yin-na kyed ynyis-ka ṅo-mtsar-cé (de ltar yin na khyed gnyis ka ngo mtshar che) if that is so, then both of you receive my best thanks! Mil.; yóṅs-pa ṅo-mtsar-cé (yongs pa ngo mtshar che) thanks to you for your coming! Mil.

Cs. also tsal (tshal) vermilion, used (among the rest) inst. of red ink for writing; mtsal-pár (mtshal par) a printing with red ink Cs.; mtsal-lcògs-pa (?) (mtshal lcogs pa) Sch.: 'clear vermilion'(?); sku-mtsál (sku mtshal) resp. for krag (khrag) blood Cs.

(W. *tsogs* (tshogs)) similar, like, equal, ḱa-dog (kha dog) as to colour S. O., sṅa-ma daṅ (snga ma dang) like the former, bdud-rtsir (bdud rtsir) like nectar S.g.; bdud daṅ mtsuṅs (bdud dang mtshungs) you are to me like a satan, you are a satan to me Pth.; lhai sdug-bsṅál daṅ ča-mtsuṅs-pai stéṅ-du (lha'i sdug bsngal dang cha mtshungs pa'i steng du) besides their sharing all the imperfections of the gods Thgy.; dús-mtsuṅs-pa (dus mtshungs pa) a contemporary Mil.; mtsuṅs-méd (mtshungs med), mtsuṅs-brál (mtshungs bral), without an equal, matchless, incomparable; sems daṅ mtsuṅs ldán-pa (sems dang mtshungs ldan pa) explained by Was. (241) as: manifestations of mind, those outward signs by which the mind manifests itself as existing.

(Zam. = Ssk. क्रव्य, raw flesh) 1. Cs.: meat for the manes of the dead, ytóṅ-ba (gtong ba) to bring an offering to the dead, skyel-ba (skyel ba) to send one; mtsun-ytór (mtshun gtor) explained in Wdṅ. by śi-bai dón-du ytór-ma ytóṅ-ba (shi ba'i don du gtor ma gtong ba); mtsun-ytór stér-ba (mtshun gtor ster ba) Wdṅ. — 2. Sch.: tutelar deities, household-gods, or rather the souls of ancestors; so Dzl. 2°, 16 (another reading is btsun (btsun)); also in mtsun-ytor (mtshun gtor), if mtsun (mtshun) be taken as a dat., it may have this signification; mes-mtsún (mes mtshun) household-gods of the Shamans Sch.

the lower part of the face, nose and mouth, the muzzle of animals Mil.; bill, beak Sch.; W. *nám-tsul* (nam tshul) nose; mtsúl-pa °gag (mtshul pa 'gag) the effect of the gall entering the nose (?) Mṅg.; ḱa-mtsúl (kha mtshul) (W. *ḱam-tsúl* (kham tshul)) face, seldom in B.
n. of a medicinal herb S.g.

(W. *tsag-túg* (tsag tug)) twins, bu mtse-ma ynyis dus rčig-na ḱruṅs-so (bu mtshe ma gnyis dus gcig na khrungs so) Pth. two twin-sons were born simultaneously; mtsé-ma ysúm-po (mtshe ma gsum po) three-twin-child, trigemini Wdṅ.
Sch.: dur-mtséd (dur mtshed), place for burning the dead.
a small lake, mtso daṅ mtseu (mtsho dang mtshe'u) lakes and lakelets Pth.

1. lake, frq. — 2. for rgya-mtso (rgya mtsho) sea, rarely. — 3. symb. num.: 4. — Comp. mtso-dkyil (mtsho dkyil), mtso-dbús (mtsho dbus) the middle of a lake. — mtso-°kór (mtsho 'khor) an assemblage of many lakes Cs. — mtso-°kyóms (mtsho 'khyoms) v. °kyoms ('khyoms). — mtso-°grám (mtsho 'gram), mtso-mtá (mtsho mtha') border of a lake. — mtso-snòn (mtsho sngon) Glr., *sóg-po tso-ṅón* (sog po tsho sngon) C. the blue lake, Kokonor, in Mongolia. — mtso-čú (mtsho chu) water,
